original_abstract:
This review summarizes recent advancements in the theoretical understanding of novel bonding modes for carbon and its heavier group 14 congeners (Si–Pb). Central to this discussion is the identification of tetrylones (EL₂), a class of divalent E(0) compounds—including carbones, silylones, and germylones—that are electronically distinct from traditional tetrylenes (ER₂). Unlike tetrylenes, which feature electron-sharing bonds and a single lone pair, tetrylones function as bidentate Lewis acids where a naked tetrele atom in the singlet ¹D state binds two σ-donor ligands through donor–acceptor interactions, thereby retaining two lone pairs. Charge- and energy decomposition analyses (EDA-NOCV) quantify these unique electronic structures, providing a rationale for their distinct chemical reactivity and supporting the expanding library of experimentally isolated species like carbodicarbenes and silylones.

The review further extends these bonding concepts to transition metal complexes [TM]–E featuring terminal, naked group 14 atoms. Bonding analyses reveal that these complexes are best described by a combination of σ and π_|_ electron-sharing bonds alongside π_|| back-donation, effectively characterizing the tetrele atom as a formal four-electron donor. Theoretical results emphasize the isolobal relationship between these carbon complexes and carbon monoxide, positioning them as potent ligands for auxiliary Lewis acids. By bridging the gap between fundamental electronic structure theory and synthetic chemistry, this work provides a comprehensive framework for predicting the stability and reactivity of as-yet unknown main group and transition metal tetrele species.
humanized_abstract:
This review covers recent developments in the theoretical concept of unconventional bonding towards carbon and its heavier group 14 congeners, Si–Pb. The diversity of tetrylones (EL₂) such as divalent carbones, silylones, and germylones are described. They are distinct from the tetrylenes (ER₂) of long standing interest in that they are not electron sharing species with a single unshared electron pair, as predicted, but rather act as bidentate Lewis acids, featuring a ground state, tetrel centred singlet ¹D species with two unshared pairs of electrons that engages through strong donor−acceptor bonding with two σ-donating ligands. Charge and energy decomposition analysis within the framework of EDA-NOCV explains the unique character of the tetrylone bond, and also demonstrates in detail the reason why these species display distinct reactivity towards various functional groups of interest, including full proof of the existence of a large number of experimentally characterized species like carbodicarbenes and their silyl substituted analogues, i.e. the silylones.

The review extends the discussion of the bonding in TM-ECs, TM being transition metal, to TM-E complexes containing naked terminal group 14 tetrele atoms: The general model is applicable to σ and π_|_ electron-sharing bonds plus π_|| back-bonding to describe a tetrele atom as a formal four-electron donor and the resulting calculation results convincingly show the isolobal relationship between carbon and carbon monoxide and consequently that carbon species are efficient ligands for auxiliary Lewis acids. The review bridges the gap between electronic structure theory and the synthetic aspect of main group and transition metal tetrele chemistry and serves as a valuable tool to infer the properties of complexes yet to be synthesized.

document.output:
This review covers recent developments in the theoretical concept of unconventional bonding towards carbon and its heavier group 14 congeners, Si–Pb. This review summarizes recent advancements in the theoretical understanding of novel bonding modes for carbon and its heavier group 14 congeners (Si–Pb).
